Framing Spain’s €1,000 cash limit and crypto reporting rules as “criminalization” misses the policy rationale. The cash cap (mainly for business transactions) targets tax evasion and the shadow economy — a long-standing structural issue in Spain. When large transactions move into traceable channels, it broadens the tax base, reduces unfair competition from undeclared operators, and increases public revenue without raising rates on compliant workers.

Crypto reporting rules serve a similar function. They don’t ban ownership or payments; they align digital assets with existing anti–money laundering and tax compliance frameworks. That reduces fraud risk, improves market integrity, and protects retail participants from opaque schemes. Regulatory clarity also lowers systemic risk by integrating crypto into supervised financial oversight rather than leaving it in a legal gray zone.

For ordinary people, the benefits are practical: more predictable public finances, fairer competition for small businesses that play by the rules, and reduced criminal financing. Everyday cash use remains legal, and digital payment options expand convenience and consumer protections. The trade-off is increased transparency — but in exchange, you get stronger institutions and a more level economic playing field.

You’re mixing vibes with economics.

Yes, the US had $10k bills in 1971. Cool trivia. They were used for bank transfers, not normal people buying houses with a briefcase of cash. They got phased out because electronic settlement made them pointless — and because giant anonymous cash instruments are amazing for crime. That’s not “feudalism,” that’s basic financial plumbing evolving.

Same with anonymous bank accounts in 90s Europe. Who benefited from those? Not the average wagecuck. It was tax dodgers, oligarch types, organized crime. Transparency rules didn’t make housing expensive — zoning bottlenecks, low interest rate bubbles, urban demand, and supply constraints did.

Cash limits and crypto reporting don’t magically create inequality. Housing affordability is about supply, credit cycles, and asset inflation — not whether you can slap €20k in paper on a table.

You can argue about privacy trade-offs, sure. But calling AML rules “neofeudalism” while ignoring monetary policy and housing policy is just aesthetic outrage. Different levers, different problems.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Third_place
>In sociology, the third place refers to the social surroundings that are separate from the two usual social environments of home ("first place") and workplace ("second place"). Examples of third places include churches, cafes, bars, clubs, libraries, gyms, bookstores, hackerspaces, stoops, parks, and theaters, among others. In his book The Great Good Place (1989), Ray Oldenburg argues that third places are important for democracy, civic engagement, and a sense of place. Oldenburg's coauthor Karen Christensen argues in the 2023 edition that third places are the answer to loneliness, political polarization, and climate resilience. She also clarifies the difference between third places and public spaces.[1]

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rite_of_passage
>A rite of passage is a ceremony or ritual of the passage which occurs when an individual leaves one group to enter another. It involves a significant change of status in society. In cultural anthropology, the term is the anglicisation of rite de passage, a French term innovated by the ethnographer Arnold van Gennep in his work Les rites de passage, The Rites of Passage.[1] The term is now fully adopted into anthropology as well as into the literature and popular cultures of many modern languages.

Die Neue These
This version is considered "More faithful to the sequence of events in the novel"

Story progression: Strictly following the narration in the main novel. Content from the expansion pack (Gaiden) is rarely mixed in, allowing the story to be told concisely and according to the timeline that the author laid out from the beginning.
Points of difference: Some action scenes or tactics have been modified to look more modern and memorable to fit the era.

OVA (1988 - 1997)
Expansion of content: Content from the expansion novel (Gaiden) was introduced into the main story from the beginning. And add scenes that are not in the novel (Anime Original) to expand the dimensions of the characters and the world.
Characters: Some characters are made to seem a little "nice" or "smarter" than in the novel to make them easier for viewers to root for (such as Reinhardt's decision in the Westerland Incident to be more cold-blooded in the novel and later versions).

I don't see how pointing out an instance of the Dunning-Kruger effect in action implies anything about the person doing that (the pointing out), because it doesn't take a lot to notice this effect and being aware of it - it's pretty common knowledge by now, and also a common occurrence, consequently it gets mentioned a lot.
Ignorance about their own ignorance is a common feature of the ignorant.
Now of course it's extremely cringe if people moan about it when that is not justified, because then they're exhibiting it themselves; but most instances of this I have seen I suspect were bait instead of outright stupidity.
It's definitely a bit tired and pointless now that it keeps getting mentioned with increasing frequency, but as long as it's justified it's difficult to complain about it. Fundamentally people being aware of it is a positive thing; as long as the people using it are aware of their own failings. Very easy to have a stupid opinion or wrong perception of something one isn't well educated on.
